name: Charlie Christian
abstract: Charles Henry Christian (July 29, 1916 – March 2, 1942) was an American swing and jazz guitarist. He was among the first electric guitarists and was a key figure in the development of bebop and cool jazz.

about_quotes.speaker: Kenny Burrell
about_quotes.evidence: I heard him playing the guitar... Charlie Christian for the single lines... we loved Charlie Christian and loved Oscar Moore, and that was one of the things my brother and I shared. Because when he came back from the army... We shared that love and admiration for those guys and would listen to the r
about_quotes.doc_title: Kenny Burrell — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Herb Ellis
about_quotes.evidence: Well Charlie Christian, I mean when I first heard him I was just absolutely stunned... So when I heard him and it got to me so then I started playing from playing a million notes to playing almost no notes.
about_quotes.doc_title: Herb Ellis interviewed by Michael Woods, Los Angeles, California, September 3, 1995 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Dave Bennett
about_quotes.evidence: I think I was reading where Lionel Hampton said that the days that he’d be playing with Benny, and it was when Charlie Christian was with the band.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dave Bennett interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, April 16, 2025

about_quotes.speaker: Steve Buckingham,
about_quotes.evidence: He was the guy who integrated the Goodman band in 1935 by getting him to hire Teddy Wilson and then Lionel Hampton and then Charlie Christian.
about_quotes.doc_title: Steve Buckingham, interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, February 25, 2019 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Buckingham
about_quotes.evidence: I started listening and it doesn’t take a genius to figure out that’s Lester Young trading off of Charlie Christian.

about_quotes.speaker: Peter Appleyard
about_quotes.evidence: I revered them because of my knowledge of Teddy Wilson and Lionel Hampton and Charlie Christian, Louis Armstrong.
about_quotes.doc_title: Peter Appleyard interviewed by Monk Rowe, Utica, New York, October 4, 2012
